English

hello I'm phae-phae and welcome to the
English we speak and hello I'm Rob
you're looking pleased with yourself Rob
oh I am I've just booked these
dirt-cheap I mean really cheap flights
to Ibiza wonderful look at the price 20
pounds returned that means extra money
to spend on food drink and partying hold
on the basic price is 20 pounds but look
at the tax you have to pay on top 100
pounds huh let me see your right I hate
paying tax I guess you could say it's a
necessary evil
hmm you could say that if you mean it's
something bad that can't be avoided and
you have to accept it in order to
achieve a good result that's exactly
what I meant Rob if it can't be avoided
like paying tax on a plane ticket then
it's unnecessary evil
let's hear some examples shall we for me
exams are a necessary evil you have to
do them to get a place at university
sitting in traffic for hours is a
necessary evil if I want to drive to the
seaside on a public holiday I hate
running but having to exercise is a
necessary evil if I want to stay fit and
healthy
this is the English we speak from BBC
learning english and we're finding out
about the phrase unnecessary evil it
describes something that you have to
accept in order to achieve something
else and Rob that tax on your plane
ticket is unavoidable
so not so cheap now is it no less money
for eating drinking and partying can you
get a refund stay at home and eat drink
and party I could but if I stayed at
home there's another necessary evil I
have to deal with what's that I'll have
to spend more time with you mmm what
time is your flight do exactly what did
I say something wrong
bye bye bye the English we speak from
BBC learning English
